title: Renal Replacement Therapy and Conservative Management for Chronic Kidney Disease Stages 4 and 5
summary:
This NICE guideline provides recommendations for managing chronic kidney disease (CKD) stages 4 and 5. It covers indications for starting renal replacement therapy (dialysis), preparing for this treatment, choosing the appropriate modality, planning dialysis access, switching or stopping therapy, and coordinating care. The guideline also addresses diet, fluids, information, education, and support.
It recommends following referral criteria for CKD and considering dialysis when indicated by the individual patient's clinical needs, including symptoms like fatigue, shortness of breath, and swelling.
The guideline emphasizes the importance of patient involvement in decision-making and provides information on how to use NICE guidelines for making informed choices about care.
It also covers the need for regular reviews and the importance of coordinating care among different healthcare professionals.
The guideline provides recommendations for research, including key research questions and rationale, and outlines context and further information resources.
It also mentions specific recommendations for research, including indications for starting renal replacement therapy, when to assess for preparation, how to assess for preparation, choosing modalities, planning access, indications for switching/stopping therapy, and recognizing symptoms.
It covers diet and fluids, frequency of review, decision support interventions, and coordinating care.
The guideline also includes information on context, finding more information, committee details, and update information.
It states that this guideline replaces previous guidelines (CG125 and TA48) and should be read in conjunction with another guideline (QS76).
It also provides information on how to report adverse events related to medicines or medical devices.
The document emphasizes the responsibility of healthcare professionals and providers to apply the guideline recommendations in the context of individual patient needs and local priorities, while also considering equality of opportunity and reducing health inequalities.
It also highlights the need to promote environmentally sustainable healthcare and reduce the environmental impact of implementing NICE recommendations.

front_matter:
## Renal replacement therapy and conservative management

NICE guideline

Published: 3 October 2018

www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng107

## Your responsibility

The recommendations in this guideline represent the view of NICE, arrived at after careful consideration of the evidence available. When exercising their judgement, professionals and practitioners are expected to take this guideline fully into account, alongside the individual needs, preferences and values of their patients or the people using their service. It is not mandatory to apply the recommendations, and the guideline does not override the responsibility to make decisions appropriate to the circumstances of the individual, in consultation with them and their families and carers or guardian.

All problems (adverse events) related to a medicine or medical device used for treatment or in a procedure should be reported to the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency using the Yellow Card Scheme.

Local commissioners and providers of healthcare have a responsibility to enable the guideline to be applied when individual professionals and people using services wish to use it. They should do so in the context of local and national priorities for funding and developing services, and in light of their duties to have due regard to the need to eliminate unlawful discrimination, to advance equality of opportunity and to reduce health inequalities. Nothing in this guideline should be interpreted in a way that would be inconsistent with complying with those duties.

Commissioners and providers have a responsibility to promote an environmentally sustainable health and care system and should assess and reduce the environmental impact of implementing NICE recommendations wherever possible.
